column2: You connect the BMP388 sensor to your Arduino over I2C, then read pressure data and convert it into altitude and temperature values. Step by step you learn how to calibrate the sensor, apply simple filtering and display results on the serial monitor or a small screen. By the end you have a compact altimeter that can be reused in weather stations, drones or hiking tools.
column5: Build an Arduino BMP388 altimeter for pressure and height
column11: BMP388 Alitimeter (Altitude, Pressure, Temperature) sensor with Arduino

column2: This project shows how to wire one or more temperature sensors and a small OLED display to your Arduino so you can see live readings. You set up the I2C bus, format text and icons and refresh the screen without flicker. The tutorial finishes with ideas for adding min and max values so your compact thermometer becomes a useful reference on your bench.
column5: Create an Arduino OLED display for temperature sensors
column11: Arduino with OLED Display and Temperature Sensors

column2: Using an Arduino and a LoRa radio module, you build a link that can send sensor readings far beyond normal Wi‑Fi or Bluetooth range. You configure frequency, addresses and data packets, then send test values between two nodes. Finally you attach real sensors so your long‑range link can support remote weather stations or status monitoring in the field.
column5: Send Arduino sensor data long‑distance using LoRa radios

column2: Here you interface a MAX6675 thermocouple amplifier with Arduino to measure high temperatures that simple sensors cannot handle. You wire the module over SPI, read raw values and convert them into degrees Celsius or Fahrenheit. The tutorial covers calibration, noise reduction and how to log readings so you can profile heaters, soldering tools or ovens safely.
column5: Measure high temperature sources using MAX6675 and Arduino

column2: You connect the APDS9960 sensor to your Arduino and enable proximity, color and gesture detection in one compact module. After wiring and loading the library, you experiment with hand movements to trigger LEDs or actions. By the end you know how to tune thresholds and translate gestures into intuitive controls for lights, menus or interactive projects.
column5: Add gesture, color and proximity sensing to Arduino projects
column11: APDS9960 Proximity, Light, RGB, Gesture Sensor with Arduino

column2: This tutorial uses the HX711 amplifier and a load cell to build a simple weight scale with Arduino. You assemble the mechanical platform, wire the bridge sensor and amplifier, then calibrate using known masses. With basic averaging and stable code, you finish with a small digital scale that can measure objects, ingredients or forces in your experiments.
column5: Build a simple weight scale using HX711 and load cells
column11: HX711 Load Cell Amplifier with Arduino

column2: You learn how to connect an anemometer wind speed sensor to Arduino and turn its pulses into meaningful speed units. The tutorial walks through counting techniques, averaging over a time window and converting to meters per second or kilometers per hour. Once finished, you have a basic weather instrument ready for outdoor testing and data logging.
column5: Measure outdoor wind speed with an Arduino anemometer
column11: Wind Speed Sensor, Anemometer with Arduino
